one_line_takeaway: TCAP detects and removes backdoor-poisoned MLLM fine-tuning samples in a fully unsupervised, white-box way by profiling a small set of attention heads whose system/vision/text attention allocation diverges under trigger conditions, achieving near-zero attack success rate while preserving clean accuracy across diverse visual and textual trigger types, substantially outperforming the prior entropy-based BYE defense especially on globally distributed and textual triggers.
key_finding: Under Blend attacks (Table 1), TCAP achieves near-zero ASR while preserving Clean Performance across all three models and five datasets, e.g., on DocVQA with Qwen3-VL: TCAP ASR=0.33% at CP=90.57% (vs. Vanilla FT CP=89.07%/ASR=98.93%, BYE CP=90.14%/ASR=67.26%). On InternVL2.5+ScienceQA, TCAP reaches CP=96.93%/ASR=0.15%; on LLaVA-NeXT+ScienceQA, BYE collapses to CP=0.00%/ASR=100.00% while TCAP holds CP=89.44%/ASR=0.05%. Detection quality (Table 2, ScienceQA) shows BYE degrading sharply on non-patch triggers (e.g., BYE F1=0.00 on Blend with InternVL2.5, F1=2.82 on Blend with LLaVA-NeXT) whereas TCAP maintains high F1 across BadNet/Blend/SIG/WaNet/FTrojan (e.g., InternVL2.5: F1 100.00/98.34/92.11/99.20/85.24 respectively; Qwen3-VL: F1 96.43/95.04/86.81/93.41/82.93). Ablation (Table 3, F1 on Blend) shows every component is necessary: removing Head Selection drops F1 from 98.34/98.85/95.04 (InternVL2.5/LLaVA-NeXT/Qwen3-VL, full method) to 43.02/22.31/15.33; removing Layer Filter drops to 67.21/31.94/11.02; removing Adaptive Components drops to 65.57/48.53/54.25; removing EM-based Voting drops to 51.70/63.10/63.01. Textual-trigger generalization (Table 4): with a text-prefix trigger on ScienceQA, TCAP reduces ASR to 0.00% with 100.00% detection F1 across all three MLLMs; with HiddenKiller on PhD, TCAP reduces ASR substantially (e.g., InternVL2.5 CP=83.73%/ASR=23.93%/F1=87.57; Qwen3-VL CP=90.07%/ASR=0.00%/F1=99.94).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Tri-Component Attention Profiling (TCAP), an unsupervised defense that purifies backdoor-poisoned fine-tuning datasets for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). The authors first identify a phenomenon they call 'attention allocation divergence': in MLLMs fine-tuned on poisoned data, a small subset of attention heads in deeper decoder layers systematically distort the balance of attention across three functional input components -- system instructions, vision inputs, and user text queries -- regardless of the trigger's modality or spatial extent (patch-based, global-blend, textual, or syntactic). They characterize two complementary head behaviors: 'System-Suppressed, Vision-Amplified' heads (Anomaly 1, attention shifts to the trigger region while suppressing system instructions) and 'System-Amplified, Vision-Suppressed' heads (Anomaly 2, heads decouple from vision and hyper-focus on system instructions). Building on this, TCAP operates in three stages: (1) extract cross-modal attention weights from the first generated token to all preceding tokens, for every head/layer, decomposed into system/vision/text sums; (2) fit an adaptive Gaussian Mixture Model (1-5 components, selected via AIC) per head on the system-instruction attention mass across training samples, restricted to the last Lsens layers, and rank heads by a 'Separation Score' (reciprocal of the overlap area between a minority 'target' Gaussian group and the background group) to select Top-K trigger-responsive heads; (3) for each selected head, cast a binary vote per sample based on posterior probability of belonging to the anomalous Gaussian component exceeding a threshold tau_vote, then aggregate votes across heads via a Dawid-Skene EM model (treating heads as noisy annotators) to produce a final per-sample poisoning posterior; samples with posterior > 0.5 are flagged and removed, and the model is retrained on the cleaned subset.
threat_model: Fine-Tuning-as-a-Service (FTaaS): a service provider fine-tunes an MLLM (via LoRA) on user-submitted, domain-specific data without necessarily vetting it. An attacker poisons a subset (10% poisoning rate used throughout) of the submitted training data by embedding a trigger into the image and/or text and relabeling the target sample's answer to an attacker-chosen output (a unified 'Backdoor Attack!' string in these experiments). The trained/fine-tuned model behaves normally on clean inputs but emits the attacker's target output whenever the trigger is present at inference time. The defender is the FTaaS operator: they have white-box access to the fine-tuned model's attention weights and the (mixed clean+poisoned) training data, but no clean reference dataset, no poison labels, and no external auxiliary modules -- i.e., an unsupervised, internal-signal-only detection setting.
limitations: The paper's own theoretical analysis (Section 3.2) explains why the prior baseline BYE fails on global/blended triggers (entropy does not drop for globally diffused triggers as it does for spatially localized patches), and empirically their own Table 1/2 results show TCAP's advantage is largest precisely against those trigger types BYE cannot handle -- but the paper does not present an analogous theoretical failure-mode analysis for TCAP itself. TCAP's own residual detection is imperfect on some settings (e.g., WaNet F1=81.64 and FTrojan F1=88.23 on LLaVA-NeXT are noticeably lower than its BadNet/Blend F1 on the same model), and residual ASR after cleaning is non-zero in several cells (e.g., InternVL2.5+DocVQA ASR=2.84%, Qwen3-VL+ScienceQA ASR=15.62%), which the paper reports but does not further analyze as a limitation. The method assumes a single, unified backdoor target output and a fixed 10% poisoning rate across all main experiments; sensitivity to poisoning rate or multiple simultaneous triggers/targets is not evaluated in the visible text. The approach also requires white-box access to internal attention maps of the fine-tuned model, restricting applicability to the FTaaS-operator threat model described (not usable by an external/black-box user of the model).

quantitative_results: InternVL2.5+ScienceQA+Blend: TCAP CP=96.93%, ASR=0.15% vs Vanilla FT CP=96.88%, ASR=93.60%
Qwen3-VL+DocVQA+Blend: TCAP CP=90.57%, ASR=0.33% vs BYE CP=90.14%, ASR=67.26%
LLaVA-NeXT+ScienceQA+Blend: BYE collapses to CP=0.00%, ASR=100.00%; TCAP CP=89.44%, ASR=0.05%
Detection F1 on ScienceQA (InternVL2.5), TCAP vs BYE: BadNet 100.00 vs 97.87, Blend 98.34 vs 0.00, SIG 92.11 vs 9.94, WaNet 99.20 vs 4.01, FTrojan 85.24 vs 9.58

Ablation F1 (Blend attack) full TCAP: 98.34/98.85/95.04 (InternVL2.5/LLaVA-NeXT/Qwen3-VL); w/o Head Selection: 43.02/22.31/15.33; w/o Layer Filter: 67.21/31.94/11.02; w/o Adaptive Components: 65.57/48.53/54.25; w/o EM-based Voting: 51.70/63.10/63.01
Textual trigger (prefix 'Hello!') on ScienceQA: TCAP ASR=0.00%, F1=100.00% across all three MLLMs
HiddenKiller trigger on PhD: TCAP CP/ASR/F1 = 83.73%/23.93%/87.57 (InternVL2.5), 85.90%/6.77%/92.95 (LLaVA-NeXT), 90.07%/0.00%/99.94 (Qwen3-VL)
